analysis:
- Strong designs (heuristics 3–6) share a clear, effective pattern: Pareto-filter to cut candidates, compute a cheap box-volume proxy, form a small proxy-ranked shortlist, run a lazy-greedy/CELF selection with cached marginal gains and a hard cap on HV calls, then apply a bounded local-search swap refinement. This balances HV quality and runtime by drastically reducing expensive HV evaluations.
- Weaker designs (1,2 and 7–10 variants) either rely on many raw HV computations (Heuristic 2 recomputes HV for every remove/replace, Heuristic 1 uses many random flips), or perform unbounded per-iteration HV work (no robust shortlist, no eval caps). That causes O(n*k) or worse HV calls and heavy Python-loop overhead.
- Key efficiency wins in better heuristics: (a) aggressive candidate reduction (nondominated + top-by-proxy), (b) a CELF-style heap with timestamps to avoid recomputing marginal gains, (c) explicit limits on HV evaluations and shortlist sizes, (d) caching and reusing computed hv_current instead of recomputing from scratch, (e) vectorized proxy computations (box volumes, distances) and operating on indices not copies.
- Repeated avoidable overheads across weaker variants: frequent vstacking and array reallocations, recomputing identical hv on same selected set, naive O(n^2) nondomination loops without early pruning, lack of normalization safeguards, and rebuilding candidate lists inside inner loops. These increase Python overhead even when HV itself is fast.
- Missed algorithmic opportunities: using exclusive/exact marginal formulas or incremental HV updates, tighter upper bounds (per-dimension boxes) to prune, maintaining marginal-gain caches across iterations, and exploiting sorted orderings to skip many HV calls. Also, returning indices instead of point copies and careful deterministic padding avoids subtle correctness/performance costs.
experience: Prefer pareto + proxy shortlist + CELF with cached timestamps and strict HV-eval budgets. Vectorize all proxy work, operate on indices, reuse hv_current and marginal caches, cap shortlist size adaptively (O(k) or fixed bound), then apply a tiny first‑improvement swap search. Add incremental/exclusive-HV routines or C/C++ bindings to cut repeated HV overhead.
current_reflection: Prefer pareto + proxy shortlist + CELF with cached timestamps and strict HV-eval budgets. Vectorize all proxy work, operate on indices, reuse hv_current and marginal caches, cap shortlist size adaptively (O(k) or fixed bound), then apply a tiny first‑improvement swap search. Add incremental/exclusive-HV routines or C/C++ bindings to cut repeated HV overhead.
good_reflections: Prefer a hybrid constructive: filter dominated points, approximate contributions with vectorized Monte‑Carlo or fast tensor proxies to shortlist candidates, then apply exact HV only on the shortlist with cached/incremental updates and a small swap-based local search. Vectorize dominance precomputation, avoid rebuilding objects in loops, and keep all heavy work batched.

Next generation heuristics should: (1) pareto‑filter then shortlist by cheap proxy; (2) compute exact marginal HVC only on that shortlist using cached/incremental HV data structures; (3) vectorize or Monte‑Carlo only when needed; (4) cap HV evaluations and local search adaptively; and (5) reuse pygmo objects and add deterministic seeding and parallel shortlist evaluation.
bad_reflections: Favor Pareto filtering + cheap box‑volume/diversity proxies + CELF lazy greedy with timestamped cached marginals, an adaptive shortlist cap (O(k) or a small constant), and a tiny bounded swap refinement. Minimize HV calls by reusing data structures, avoid per‑candidate full HV scans, vectorize dominance checks, and bound total HV evaluations.

Pareto‑filter first, cap candidates to O(max(k,100)), precompute box‑volume proxies, then run a CELF lazy greedy with a timestamped marginal cache and strict HV‑call budget. Reuse contiguous arrays and a single hv wrapper (avoid rebuilding pygmo objects per call). Finish with a tiny bounded first‑improvement swap among top non‑selected. Make shortlist sizes and budgets adaptive to k for a consistent tradeoff between HV quality and runtime.

Favor a CELF lazy greedy on a Pareto-filtered, box‑volume shortlisted pool. Cache marginal gains with timestamps, cap candidate set to O(max(k, c)), vectorize dominance/distance ops, and limit HV calls with provable upper bounds. Use a tiny focused swap local search, deterministic seeds, and parallel evaluation for the remaining expensive HV computations.
comprehensive_reflection:
- Keywords: Pareto-filter, proxy shortlist, CELF lazy greedy, timestamped marginal cache, incremental/exclusive HV, vectorization, adaptive shortlist, swap local search, C/C++ bindings.
- Advice: Pareto-filter, build a vectorized proxy shortlist, run CELF with timestamped cached marginals and incremental HV updates, cap shortlist/adapt HV budgets (O(k) or fixed), batch/index all ops, then run a tiny bounded first‑improvement swap; reuse hv_current, marginal caches and native HV bindings for hot paths.
- Avoid: per‑candidate full HV recomputes, rebuilding objects inside loops, unvectorized per‑point proxies, unbounded HV evaluations, and nondeterministic seeding.
- Explanation: Minimizes costly HV calls by shortlisting, caching and incremental updates, keeping heavy work batched so runtime scales predictably while preserving solution quality.
